findings.description: Docstring brags 'strictly' but strptime('%Y-%m-%d') cheerfully swallows non-padded inputs like '2024-1-5' and zero-padded years like '0024-01-01', so the strictness contract is a polite fiction.
day_range fully materializes one date object per day with no cap, so a multi-century span (e.g. date(1,1,1)..date(9999,12,31)) allocates ~3.65M objects instead of yielding lazily.
business_days_between only needs a count yet inherits day_range's full-list allocation, turning an O(1)-ish arithmetic problem into O(n) memory for wide ranges.

clamp_to_range never validates tz-awareness, so mixing naive and aware datetimes throws a raw TypeError from min/max instead of the clean ValueError that humanize_delta bothers to raise — inconsistent guarding.
int() truncation toward zero can collapse a sub-second-negative delta to 0, reporting 'just now' for a moment that is technically in the future.
humanize_delta's granularity ceiling stops at weeks, so a two-year-old timestamp proudly announces '104 w ago' instead of months/years (arguably fine under 'coarse', hence a nit).
